name: sketch-part-agent-vector-drawing
description: Train a multi-modal LLM agent for part-level vector sketch generation using multi-turn process-reward RL. Generate sketches incrementally with visual feedback at each step, producing interpretable and locally-editable results. Use when you need controllable, compositional sketch generation where each part can be modified independently.
paperTitle: Teaching an Agent to Sketch One Part at a Time
content:
## Overview

This skill teaches agents to generate vector sketches by decomposing the task into semantic parts. Rather than generating entire sketches at once, the agent generates one part at a time with visual feedback after each part. The key innovation is ControlSketch-Part, an automatically-annotated dataset with rich part-level labels, combined with multi-turn process-reward reinforcement learning that provides feedback on sketch quality at each generation step.

## Core Concepts

**Part-Level Generation Architecture**
- Agent generates sketch parts sequentially (e.g., head, body, limbs)
- Multi-turn interaction: each part includes visual rendering and feedback
- Agent observes current partial sketch before generating next part
- Results in interpretable, hierarchically-structured output

**Process-Reward RL Framework**
- Reward signal at each part (process reward), not just final output
- Provides learning signal for part-quality, not just overall coherence
- Enables agent to learn good part-generation procedures
- Supervised fine-tuning provides initial behavior, RL refines it

**ControlSketch-Part Dataset**
- Automatic annotation pipeline segments vector sketches into parts
- Semantic part labels: clothing, body parts, accessories, etc.
- Rich annotations enable fine-grained learning
- Structured multi-stage labeling captures part hierarchies

**Multi-Modal LLM Agent**
- Vision input: rendered partial sketch as image
- Text input: part specification, style constraints
- Text output: SVG path commands for next part
- Rendering closed-loop: generated part immediately visible to agent

## When To Use

- Generating vector sketches from text descriptions
- Fashion design where each component (garment, accessory) edits independently
- Character design where body parts can be modified individually
- Educational tools where sketch building shows step-by-step process
- Interactive sketch generation with user feedback
- Producing interpretable sketches where parts have semantic meaning
- Controllable generation: modify specific parts without regeneration

## When NOT to Use

- Photorealistic or highly detailed raster graphics
- Real-time interactive drawing (process reward RL adds latency)
- Sketches with complex interdependencies (parts tightly coupled)
- Generating full scenes with complex spatial reasoning
- Tasks requiring pixel-perfect alignment (vector sketches are stylized)
- Low-resource settings (requires large annotated dataset and RL training)

## Architecture: Part-Based Agent Generation

Structure the agent for sequential part generation with closed-loop feedback:

```python
class SketchPartAgent:
    """
    Multi-modal LLM agent that generates vector sketches part-by-part
    with process-reward RL for each generation step.
    """

    def __init__(self, llm_model, tokenizer, device='cuda'):
        self.llm = llm_model  # Multi-modal LLM (e.g., GPT-4V, Claude)
        self.tokenizer = tokenizer
        self.device = device
        self.renderer = SVGRenderer()
        self.part_predictor = PartPredictor()  # Predicts next part type
        self.process_reward_model = ProcessRewardModel()

    def generate_sketch(self, description, num_parts=None, style=None):
        """
        Generate complete vector sketch by generating parts sequentially.

        Args:
            description: text description of sketch (e.g., "a woman wearing a blue dress")
            num_parts: target number of parts (if None, agent decides)
            style: optional style constraint (e.g., "minimalist", "detailed")

        Returns:
            sketch: dict with parts and full SVG
            generation_log: record of generation process for interpretability
        """
        self.sketch = {
            'parts': [],
            'part_names': [],
            'svg_paths': []
        }
        self.generation_log = []

        # Initial context from description
        system_prompt = self.build_system_prompt(style)
        user_message = f"Generate a sketch of: {description}"

        # Multi-turn generation loop
        turn = 0
        max_turns = num_parts or 10

        while turn < max_turns:
            # Predict next part type
            next_part_name = self.predict_next_part(
                description,
                self.sketch['part_names']
            )

            if next_part_name == '[DONE]':
                # Agent decided sketch is complete
                break

            # Generate SVG path for this part
            svg_path, generation_details = self.generate_part(
                description=description,
                partial_sketch=self.sketch,
                next_part_name=next_part_name,
                system_prompt=system_prompt
            )

            # Render part onto canvas
            rendered_image = self.renderer.add_path(
                svg_path,
                to_canvas=True
            )

            # Compute process reward: quality of this part
            part_reward = self.process_reward_model.evaluate(
                part_svg=svg_path,
                part_name=next_part_name,
                context_image=rendered_image,
                full_description=description
            )

            # Store in sketch
            self.sketch['parts'].append(svg_path)
            self.sketch['part_names'].append(next_part_name)
            self.sketch['svg_paths'].append(svg_path)

            # Log for RL training
            self.generation_log.append({
                'turn': turn,
                'part_name': next_part_name,
                'svg_path': svg_path,
                'process_reward': part_reward,
                'rendered_image': rendered_image,
                'generation_details': generation_details
            })

            # Update user message for next turn (closed-loop)
            user_message = self.build_followup_prompt(
                rendered_image,
                next_part_name,
                part_reward
            )

            turn += 1

        # Finalize sketch
        final_svg = self.renderer.export_svg()
        self.sketch['final_svg'] = final_svg

        return self.sketch, self.generation_log

    def predict_next_part(self, description, completed_parts):
        """
        Predict which part should be generated next.
        Uses semantic understanding to order parts logically.
        """
        # Query LLM: given description and completed parts, what's next?
        context = {
            'description': description,
            'completed_parts': completed_parts,
            'num_completed': len(completed_parts)
        }

        prompt = f"""
        Given this sketch description: "{description}"
        Already generated parts: {', '.join(completed_parts)}

        What should be the next part to add? Choose from logical components.
        If the sketch is complete, respond with [DONE].
        """

        next_part = self.llm.generate_text(prompt, max_tokens=20)
        return next_part.strip()

    def generate_part(self, description, partial_sketch, next_part_name,
                     system_prompt):
        """
        Generate SVG path for a specific part with full context.

        Args:
            description: overall sketch description
            partial_sketch: current sketch with generated parts
            next_part_name: semantic name of part to generate
            system_prompt: instruction for the agent

        Returns:
            svg_path: SVG path string (e.g., "M 10 10 L 50 50 Z")
            details: metadata about generation
        """
        # Build vision context: render current partial sketch
        if partial_sketch['parts']:
            current_canvas = self.renderer.render_parts(partial_sketch['parts'])
        else:
            current_canvas = self.renderer.create_blank_canvas()

        # Build multimodal prompt
        vision_input = current_canvas
        text_input = f"""
        Sketch description: {description}
        Already drawn: {', '.join(partial_sketch['part_names'])}
        Next part to draw: {next_part_name}

        Generate SVG path commands for the {next_part_name} part.
        The part should:
        - Fit with existing parts
        - Match the overall style and description
        - Use standard SVG syntax (M, L, C, Z, etc.)
        - Return only the path string, no explanation

        SVG Path:
        """

        # Call multimodal LLM
        svg_path = self.llm.generate_with_vision(
            vision_input=vision_input,
            text_input=text_input,
            max_tokens=200
        )

        # Validate SVG
        svg_path = self.validate_and_fix_svg(svg_path)

        return svg_path, {
            'current_canvas': current_canvas,
            'part_name': next_part_name
        }

    def validate_and_fix_svg(self, svg_path):
        """
        Validate SVG syntax, fix common errors.
        """
        try:
            # Parse SVG
            parsed = parse_svg_path(svg_path)

            # Check validity
            if not parsed or len(parsed) == 0:
                return "M 0 0 L 10 0 L 10 10 Z"  # Fallback: small square

            # Ensure path is closed (ends with Z)
            if not svg_path.strip().endswith('Z'):
                svg_path = svg_path.strip() + ' Z'

            return svg_path

        except Exception as e:
            # On parse error, return fallback
            return "M 0 0 L 10 0 L 10 10 Z"

    def build_followup_prompt(self, rendered_image, last_part_name, part_reward):
        """
        Build next prompt that includes visual feedback.
        Closed-loop: LLM sees what it generated.
        """
        quality_feedback = {
            0.8: "well-executed",
            0.6: "acceptable but could be improved",
            0.4: "has some issues",
            0.0: "needs major revision"
        }

        quality_text = quality_feedback[
            max(k for k in quality_feedback.keys() if k <= part_reward)
        ]

        prompt = f"""
        Good! I've rendered the {last_part_name}, which looks {quality_text}.
        What part should we draw next to complete the sketch?
        """

        return prompt

    def build_system_prompt(self, style=None):
        """Build system prompt for the sketch agent."""
        base_prompt = """You are an expert vector sketch artist.
        You generate sketches part-by-part, focusing on clean, simple lines.
        Each part you generate should:
        - Use consistent line weight and style
        - Fit naturally with previously drawn parts
        - Follow standard SVG path syntax
        - Be geometrically accurate
        """

        if style:
            base_prompt += f"\nStyle guideline: {style}"

        return base_prompt
```

## Process-Reward Model for Part Evaluation

Implement rewards that guide part-level quality:

```python
class ProcessRewardModel:
    """
    Evaluates quality of individual sketch parts.
    Provides learning signal for multi-turn RL.
    """

    def __init__(self):
        # Models trained to predict part quality
        self.coherence_model = CoherenceModel()      # Fits with context
        self.validity_model = ValidityModel()        # Valid SVG + reasonable geometry
        self.style_consistency_model = StyleModel()  # Matches overall style

    def evaluate(self, part_svg, part_name, context_image, full_description):
        """
        Compute reward for generated part.

        Args:
            part_svg: SVG path string
            part_name: semantic name (e.g., "left_arm")
            context_image: rendered image of sketch so far
            full_description: overall sketch description

        Returns:
            reward: scalar in [0, 1]
        """
        rewards = {}

        # Reward 1: Coherence with existing parts
        coherence = self.evaluate_coherence(
            part_svg,
            context_image,
            part_name
        )
        rewards['coherence'] = coherence

        # Reward 2: Validity and geometric sensibility
        validity = self.evaluate_validity(part_svg, part_name)
        rewards['validity'] = validity

        # Reward 3: Style consistency
        style_match = self.evaluate_style_consistency(
            part_svg,
            context_image,
            full_description
        )
        rewards['style'] = style_match

        # Reward 4: Alignment with part semantics
        semantic_alignment = self.evaluate_semantic_alignment(
            part_svg,
            part_name,
            full_description
        )
        rewards['semantics'] = semantic_alignment

        # Weighted combination
        final_reward = (
            0.3 * coherence +
            0.2 * validity +
            0.25 * style_match +
            0.25 * semantic_alignment
        )

        return final_reward

    def evaluate_coherence(self, part_svg, context_image, part_name):
        """
        How well does this part fit with existing parts?
        Measure: spatial proximity, alignment, overlap avoidance.
        """
        # Render part on current canvas
        rendered_with_part = render_svg_on_image(part_svg, context_image)

        # Detect parts in rendered image
        part_bboxes = detect_parts(rendered_with_part)

        if len(part_bboxes) < 2:
            return 0.5  # Only one part (can't assess coherence)

        # Measure spatial relationships
        # For body parts: check that they connect logically
        # For clothing: check that they align with body

        expected_connections = get_expected_connections(part_name)

        coherence_score = 0.0
        for expected_neighbor in expected_connections:
            if expected_neighbor in part_bboxes:
                # Parts are spatially close
                distance = compute_spatial_distance(
                    part_bboxes[part_name],
                    part_bboxes[expected_neighbor]
                )

                if distance < COHERENCE_THRESHOLD:
                    coherence_score += 1.0

        # Normalize
        if expected_connections:
            coherence_score /= len(expected_connections)
        else:
            coherence_score = 1.0  # No specific coherence requirement

        return min(coherence_score, 1.0)

    def evaluate_validity(self, part_svg, part_name):
        """
        Is the SVG valid and geometrically sensible?
        Measures: parseable SVG, reasonable bounding box, non-degenerate.
        """
        try:
            # Parse SVG
            path = parse_svg_path(part_svg)

            # Check for empty path
            if not path or len(path) == 0:
                return 0.0

            # Extract bounding box
            bbox = compute_bbox(path)
            width = bbox['xmax'] - bbox['xmin']
            height = bbox['ymax'] - bbox['ymin']

            # Check for reasonable size (not too small, not too large)
            min_size = 5
            max_size = 200

            if width < min_size or height < min_size:
                return 0.3  # Too small

            if width > max_size or height > max_size:
                return 0.3  # Too large

            # Check for aspect ratio sensibility
            aspect_ratio = width / height if height > 0 else 1

            expected_ratios = get_expected_aspect_ratios(part_name)

            aspect_validity = 1.0
            for expected_min, expected_max in expected_ratios:
                if expected_min <= aspect_ratio <= expected_max:
                    aspect_validity = 1.0
                    break
            else:
                aspect_validity = 0.7  # Unusual but possible

            return aspect_validity

        except Exception:
            return 0.0  # Invalid SVG

    def evaluate_style_consistency(self, part_svg, context_image, description):
        """
        Does this part match the visual style of existing parts?
        Measures: line weight, stroke style, complexity level.
        """
        # Extract style features from existing parts
        existing_style = extract_style_features(context_image)

        # Extract style features from new part
        new_style = extract_style_features_from_svg(part_svg)

        # Compare style features
        line_weight_match = compare_line_weight(existing_style, new_style)
        complexity_match = compare_complexity(existing_style, new_style)
        symmetry_match = check_symmetry_consistency(part_svg, description)

        style_score = (
            0.4 * line_weight_match +
            0.3 * complexity_match +
            0.3 * symmetry_match
        )

        return style_score

    def evaluate_semantic_alignment(self, part_svg, part_name, description):
        """
        Does the part semantically match the description?
        E.g., if description says "blue dress", dress part should look like dress.
        """
        # Extract semantic concepts from description
        concepts = extract_concepts(description)

        # Check if part matches expected appearance
        semantic_score = 1.0

        # For body parts: rough shape validation
        if part_name in ['head', 'torso', 'left_arm', 'right_arm', 'left_leg', 'right_leg']:
            if not is_reasonable_body_shape(part_svg, part_name):
                semantic_score *= 0.7

        # For clothing: match with description
        if 'dress' in description.lower() and 'dress' in part_name.lower():
            if is_dress_like(part_svg):
                semantic_score *= 1.0
            else:
                semantic_score *= 0.6

        return semantic_score
```

## Training with Supervised Fine-Tuning + RL

Implement two-phase training:

```python
def train_sketch_part_agent(dataset, num_sft_epochs=10, num_rl_epochs=5):
    """
    Phase 1: Supervised Fine-Tuning on ControlSketch-Part dataset
    Phase 2: Process-Reward RL to optimize part-level quality
    """
    agent = SketchPartAgent(...)
    sft_optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(agent.parameters(), lr=1e-5)
    ppo_optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(agent.parameters(), lr=5e-6)

    # Phase 1: SFT on demonstration sketches
    print("Phase 1: Supervised Fine-Tuning")
    for epoch in range(num_sft_epochs):
        for batch in dataset.get_sft_batch():
            description = batch['description']
            part_sequence = batch['part_sequence']  # [part_name, svg_path, ...]

            loss_sft = 0.0
            for i, (part_name, ground_truth_svg) in enumerate(part_sequence):
                # Generate part
                predicted_svg = agent.generate_part(
                    description=description,
                    partial_sketch={'parts': part_sequence[:i], 'part_names': []},
                    next_part_name=part_name,
                    system_prompt=""
                )

                # SVG matching loss (structural similarity)
                loss = svg_matching_loss(predicted_svg, ground_truth_svg)
                loss_sft += loss

            sft_optimizer.zero_grad()
            loss_sft.backward()
            sft_optimizer.step()

    # Phase 2: Process-Reward RL (PPO)
    print("Phase 2: Process-Reward Reinforcement Learning")
    for epoch in range(num_rl_epochs):
        for batch in dataset.get_rl_batch():
            description = batch['description']

            # Generate sketch with agent
            sketch, generation_log = agent.generate_sketch(description)

            # Collect rewards from process-reward model
            returns = []
            cumulative_return = 0.0

            for log_entry in reversed(generation_log):
                cumulative_return += log_entry['process_reward']
                returns.insert(0, cumulative_return)

            # PPO update
            for i, log_entry in enumerate(generation_log):
                part_svg = log_entry['svg_path']
                advantage = returns[i] - baseline(log_entry)

                # Policy loss: encourage high-reward actions
                policy_loss = -advantage * log_prob(part_svg)

                ppo_optimizer.zero_grad()
                policy_loss.backward()
                ppo_optimizer.step()

    return agent
```

## Critical Design Decisions

**Part Order Prediction**
- Logical ordering matters: generate structural parts before details
- Body before clothing, skeleton before hair
- Implement part hierarchy and dependency tracking

**Reward Weighting**
- Coherence (0.3): fitting with context is primary
- Validity (0.2): generation must be geometrically sound
- Style (0.25): consistency across parts
- Semantics (0.25): match description intent

**SFT vs RL Balance**
- SFT: establish behavioral baseline, learning from examples
- RL: optimize process rewards, refine part generation procedure
- Combined: better generalization than either alone

**Closed-Loop Feedback**
- Critical: agent must see rendered result before next part
- Enables visual grounding and error correction
- Increases interpretability

## Results & Expected Outcomes

**Generation Quality**
- Part-level generation produces coherent, semantically meaningful sketches
- Sequential generation creates interpretable step-by-step process
- Users can edit individual parts without full regeneration

**Control and Interpretability**
- Each part labeled and identifiable
- Generation order follows logical hierarchy
- Process logs show exactly which parts were generated when

**User Interaction**
- Humans can provide feedback at each step
- Partial sketches visible and editable mid-generation
- Fine-grained control over sketch composition

## Hyperparameters & Configuration

| Parameter | Role | Typical Value | Notes |
|-----------|------|---------------|-------|
| Max Parts | Maximum parts per sketch | 8-12 | Prevents overly complex sketches |
| SFT Epochs | Supervised fine-tuning iterations | 10 | Establish behavioral baseline |
| RL Epochs | Reinforcement learning iterations | 5 | Optimize based on rewards |
| Learning Rate (SFT) | SFT optimization step | 1e-5 | Conservative for model stability |
| Learning Rate (RL) | RL optimization step | 5e-6 | Smaller for policy updates |
| Coherence Weight | Reward importance | 0.30 | Highest priority |
| Validity Weight | Reward importance | 0.20 | Ensure valid SVG |
| Style Weight | Reward importance | 0.25 | Visual consistency |
| Semantic Weight | Reward importance | 0.25 | Match description |
| SVG Max Tokens | Generation length limit | 200-300 | SVG paths typically short |
| Baseline Discount | RL return calculation | 0.99 | Standard for RL |
| PPO Epochs | Updates per sample | 3-5 | Standard PPO |
| Clip Ratio | PPO clipping parameter | 0.2 | Standard value |

## Limitations & Future Work

- Part ordering heuristics may not generalize to all sketch types
- Complex interdependent parts (e.g., interlocking shapes) challenging
- Vectorization forces discrete part boundaries (fuzzy details difficult)
- Requires large annotated dataset (ControlSketch-Part creation expensive)
- Process rewards assume parts can be independently evaluated (may not hold)

## References

- Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) methodology
- Multi-Turn Dialogue in Language Models (context management)
- Vision-Language Models for creative tasks (multimodal LLMs)
- Vector Graphics Rendering and Optimization (SVG generation)
- Hierarchical Compositional Generation (part-based decomposition)
